009                                               A BSTRACT
010
011            Despite the success of Instruction Tuning (IT) in training large language models
012            (LLMs) to perform arbitrary user-specified tasks, these models often still leverage
013            spurious or biased features learned from their training data, leading to undesired
014            behaviours when deploying them in new contexts. In this work, we introduce
015            Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT), which trains LLMs to condition their responses
016            by “focusing on” specific features whilst ignoring others, leading to different be-
017            haviours based on what features are specified. Across several experimental set-
018
               tings, we show that focus-tuned models can be adaptively steered by focusing on
               different features at inference-time: for instance, robustness can be improved by
019
               focusing on task-causal features and ignoring spurious features, and social bias
020
               can be mitigated by ignoring demographic categories. Furthermore, FIT can steer
021            behaviour in new contexts, generalising under distribution shift and to new unseen
022            features at inference time, and thereby facilitating more robust, fair, and control-
023            lable LLM applications in real-world environments.
024
025
026   1   I NTRODUCTION
027
028   Instruction Tuning (IT) (Zhang et al., 2023), a specialised form of supervised fine-tuning (SFT),
029   has become an essential step in the process of developing effective instruction-following large lan-
030   guage models (LLMs) (Ouyang et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024). While ex-
031   tensive pre-training to perform next token prediction allows LLMs to extract common patterns and
032   knowledge from large text corpora, IT fine-tunes these models on input-output pairs complemented
033
      by natural-language task instructions, teaching them to perform open-ended language-based tasks
      given instructions (Huang et al., 2023). However, despite the improvements observed in zero-shot
034
      generalisation from IT, recent studies suggest that some of these gains may be superficial, stemming
035
      from the models’ ability to learn task template formats or spurious input/output correlations rather
036   than a more generalisable instruction-following capability (Kung & Peng, 2023; Ghosh et al., 2024).
037   As a result, LLMs may fail to generalise to new contexts where the same templates or spurious cor-
038   relations are not present (Kung & Peng, 2023).
039
      To address these limitations, we propose Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT), an extension of traditional
040
      IT where LLMs are also fine-tuned with respect to an instruction regarding which features to “focus
041
      on” or “not focus on”. Training LLMs to condition responses on the provided focus specification,
042   where responses to the same input differ based on the specified features, allows end users to dy-
043   namically steer model behaviour by indicating which features should play a role or be ignored in
044   performing a task. For instance, in Figure 1, we show how FIT can be used to steer a model to ig-
045   nore gender stereotypes and focus instead on task-relevant information in order to correctly solve a
046   question-answering task. In our experiments, we demonstrate that FIT is effective at steering models
047   to ignore known spurious features while focusing on causal (task-relevant) features (see Section 4)
048   across a variety of features and tasks such as sentiment analysis and natural language inference, and
049   can be used to mitigate social bias by ignoring demographic features in question-answering. We find
050
      that FIT is also robust to distribution shifts over feature values, and can generalise to new, held-out
      features not encountered during training. In sum, our primary contributions are as follows:
051
052         1. We introduce Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT), a method that allows users to flexibly and
053            dynamically specify what features a model should or should not focus on when performing
               a task at inference time. FIT enables practitioners to incorporate domain-specific knowl-

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

063   Figure 1: Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT). In the example above, a model that is simply Instruction
064   Tuned would follow biases present in the training set. On the other hand, a FIT model can dynam-
065   ically focus or ignore specific features of the input. Here we report a sample from BBQ (Parrish
066   et al., 2022) (a question- answering dataset designed to elicit incorrect answers from models that
067   perform the task by resorting to socially biased stereotypes). Both a man and a woman are present

      pre-training and instruction tuning biases, a model could respond the man did, despite the conflict-
069

070

071
072
073               edge about causal, spurious, or bias-relevant features in order to steer models according to
074               the desired feature specification.
075
               2. We experiment with FIT across several key NLP tasks, including sentiment analysis, nat-
                  ural language inference, and question-answering. We find that FIT is highly effective for
076
                  steering behaviour on all tasks with respect to a variety of lexical, distributional, semantic,
077
                  and demographic features.
078            3. We show that FIT generalises with respect to (a) distribution shift over feature values and
079               (b) focusing on new features not seen during training.
080
081
082
      2     BACKGROUND AND R ELATED W ORK
083
      2.1      S PURIOUS F EATURE L EARNING
084
085   Deep neural networks, such as foundation models like LLMs, are susceptible to relying on spurious
086   features present in the training dataset – i.e., input features that are correlated with outputs in the
087   training distribution, but are not correlated in all test distributions (Ye et al., 2024). Relying on spu-
088   rious features leads models to fail to generalise under distribution shifts where such correlations may
089   no longer hold Wang et al. (2023a). Spurious features have been extensively studied in computer
090   vision, encompassing features such as background colour (Xiao et al., 2021; Venkataramani et al.,
091   2024; Arjovsky et al., 2019) or texture (Geirhos et al., 2018; Baker et al., 2018), and are also preva-
092   lent in many widely used NLP benchmarks (Sun et al., 2024b; Borkan et al., 2019). For instance, the
093
      token SPIELBERG is spuriously correlated with positive sentiment in datasets like SST-2 (Socher
      et al., 2013b), meaning that models trained on SST-2 may learn to predict sentiment by leveraging
094
      these spurious features instead of more general sentiment features (Wang & Culotta, 2020). This
095
      reliance on non-causal features undermines the robustness of models in generalising to distribution
096   shift.
097
098   A variety of approaches have been explored to detect and mitigate the effects of spurious feature
099
      learning, particularly under distribution shifts. Traditional approaches include prompt engineering
      (Sun et al., 2024b), regularisation techniques (Arjovsky et al., 2019; Chew et al., 2024), and di-
100
      rectly incorporating causal inference strategies (Wang & Culotta, 2020; 2021; Udomcharoenchaikit
101
      et al., 2022). Substantial work in mechanistic interpretability has also aimed to discover models’
102   representation and use of task-causal or spurious features: for instance, causal probing (which trains
103   probing classifiers to recognise and modify supervised feature representations encoded by founda-
104   tion models; see Belinkov, 2022; Canby et al., 2024; Davies & Khakzar, 2024) has been used to
105   study how models leverage causal versus spurious features features in the context of a given task
106   (Ravfogel et al., 2021; Lasri et al., 2022; Davies et al., 2023). Other works have leveraged unsuper-
107   vised mechanistic interpretability methods, such as circuit discovery techniques (Wang et al., 2023b;
      Conmy et al., 2023) and sparse auto-encoders (Subramanian et al., 2018; Yun et al., 2021), to im-


                                                                           2
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


108
      prove generalisation by discovering spurious features leveraged by models in performing a given
109   task and ablating their use of these features (Gandelsman et al., 2024; Marks et al., 2024). Finally,
110   concept removal methods locate and manipulate supervised feature representations corresponding
111   to bias features encoded by foundation models in order to remove these features (Ravfogel et al.,
112   2020; 2022; 2023; Iskander et al., 2023; Belrose et al., 2024; Kuzmin et al., 2024).
113
114
      2.2   C ONTROLLING LLM S
115
116
      Instruction Tuning. Due to the next-word prediction training objective, large language models
117   (LLMs) often struggle by default to generate outputs that align with human instructions in down-
118   stream applications (Huang et al., 2023). Instruction-tuning (IT) mitigates this issue by fine-tuning
119   pre-trained LLMs on datasets composed of instruction-response pairs (Zhang et al., 2023), aiming to
120   align the responses of the fine-tuned model more closely with the distributions preferred by humans
121   (Ouyang et al., 2022). There are several popular approaches for collecting IT training data, such as
122   using human-annotated data (Dolly, 2023), extracting datasets from existing collections (Longpre
123   et al., 2023; Mishra et al., 2022), or gathering data from internet sources (Zhou et al., 2024). IT
124   datasets can also be synthesised with LLMs, either by bootstraping them from the same model that
125
      will be instruction-tuned on them (Wang et al., 2023c; Chen et al., 2024), or by distilling from a
      larger or more powerful model to instruction-tune smaller models (Taori et al., 2023; Mitra et al.,
126
      2023; Xu et al., 2023).
127
128   Despite the success of IT in zero-shot generalisation, Gudibande et al. (2023) find that improve-
129   ments on many downstream benchmark tasks may be largely due to coverage of task data within
130   IT training datasets; and bootstrapping IT methods (which, in principle, might not be subject to this
131
      issue provided they synthesise novel IT task instances) require a robust and effective LLM for fine-
      tuning to avoid degenerate training cycles (Zhang et al., 2023). Furthermore, Kung & Peng (2023)
132
      show that some of the downstream performance gains from IT can be attributed to models’ ability
133
      to learn surface-level patterns, such as the required answer format, rather than acquiring more gen-
134   eralisable instruction-following skills. These limitations underscore the need for advancements in
135   supervised fine-tuning (SFT) methods beyond IT to facilitate more predictable and reliable control
136   of downstream model behaviours.
137
      Refocusing LLMs. Several methods have been proposed to better control instruction-tuned models
138
      both during and after training. Llama-Guard (Inan et al., 2023) fine-tunes LLMs to detect predefined
139
      risk features in inputs and outputs based on a user-specified taxonomy, such as identifying sexual
140   content in inappropriate contexts. JsonTuning (Gao et al., 2023) enhances traditional instruction
141   tuning by enforcing structured input and output formats in JSON, clarifying task requirements and
142   reducing sensitivity to paraphrasing (Sun et al., 2024a). In contrast, Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT),
143   as introduced in this work, provides a more flexible and powerful approach. While Llama-Guard
144   operates only post-training and is limited to the safety domain, FIT enables fine-grained control
145   both during and after training, conditioning models on a broader range of features across arbitrary
146   domains via natural-language specifications. Moreover, unlike JsonTuning, which is restricted to
147   enforcing output structure, FIT allows users to specify input features, enabling the model to ignore
148
      spurious correlations or highlight task-relevant attributes.
149
150
      3     M ETHODOLOGY
151
152
153
      Preliminaries. We consider a pre-trained, decoder-only large language model (LLM) pθ that models
      the probability distribution over its vocabulary V autoregressively. For an input sequence x =
154                                                                             QL
155
      [x1 , . . . , xL ] ∈ V L , the joint probability of x is given by pθ (x) = i=1 pθ (xi |x<i ), with pθ (x1 |∅) =
      pθ (x1 ). In traditional supervised learning, for a sample (x, y) ∼ D, the conditional likelihood of the
156                                               QL
157   output y given input x is pθ (y|x) = i=1 pθ (yi |x, y<i ), with pθ (y1 |∅) = pθ (y1 ); and in supervised
158
      fine-tuning (SFT) of LLMs, this manifests as minimising the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of y
      given x.
159
160   In instruction tuning (IT) (Zhang et al., 2023), a form of SFT, an additional task instruction I ac-
161   companies the input-output pair (x, y), forming a tuple (I, x, y). The objective becomes minimising
      the NLL of y given both I and x, i.e., pθ (y|I, x).

                                                            3
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


162
      Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT). We introduce Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT), a specialised form
163   of instruction tuning that trains LLMs to adjust their responses based on user-specified features
164   provided in natural language.
165
166
      Let F denote the set of possible features (e.g., specific keywords, sentiment, verb tense, demographic
      information, etc.) that the model can be instructed to focus on or ignore when generating responses.
167
      We consider a set of natural language instructions to focus or rule out specified features in F which
168
      we term the focus instruction set Ifocus .1 Explicitly, we define Ifocus as
169
170                    Ifocus = {∅, focus(Fi ), ignore(Fj ), focus(Fi ) ∧ ignore(Fj ) | Fi , Fj ∈ F},                                                    (1)
171
172   where: ∅ denotes an empty focus instruction with no features to focus on or to ignore; focus(Fi ) is an
173
      instruction to focus on feature Fi ; ignore(Fj ) is an instruction to ignore feature Fj ; and focus(Fi ) ∧
      ignore(Fj ) is an instruction to focus on feature Fi whilst ignoring feature Fj . We include the default
174
      prompt in order to aid the model in learning the underlying task as well as the ability to refocus its
175
      attention on specified features during FIT.
176
177   Consider a sample (x, y) ∼ pdata (x, y) drawn from an underlying data distribution and
178   a focus instruction Ifocus drawn from a distribution pIfocus over the set of focus instruc-
179
      tions Ifocus . Then the likelihood of response y conditioned on input x, task instruc-
      tion I (as in standard IT), and focus-instruction Ifocus is modelled as pθ (y|I, Ifocus , x).
180
181
182   FIT Training. Consider a clas- ⚙️                          Instruction: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the question below.
      sification task2 with finite label

184                                        trying to help the more youthful man to remember some details about a party but sadly it wasn't going very well...

185
      feature C ∈ F is fully predic-
      tive of label y ∈ Y given input Question: Who has trouble remembering things? (a) The old man; (b) The more youthful man; (c) Cannot answer.
186
      x at both training time and un-                🔎  -           : Focus on answering question from
                                                                                                            🔎
187                                                                                                                     -            : Focus on age.
      der distribution shift (Koh et al.,                                context alone
188   2021). We also consider spu-                                  -          : Ignore age.
                                                                                                                 -           : Don't focus on answering the
                                                                                                                    question based on the context alone.
                                             (one
189   rious features S ∈ S ⊆ F chosen)                  -                         : Focus on answering
                                                                                                               -                         : Focus on age, don't
                                                          question from the context alone, ignore age.
190   from a subset of spurious fea-                                                                          answer the question based on the context alone.

191   tures S, where feature values3
                                                                  (b) The more youthful man. ( )                              (a) The old man. (      )
192   s ∈ Image(S) for some spuri-
193   ous feature S ∈ S correlate with
194   a label ys ∈ Y, where this corre-
195   lation may change under distri- Figure 2: Example of focus labels. Focus labels for a modified
196
      bution shift (Ming et al., 2022). example from BBQ. Here, age is a spurious feature.
      Finally, we define F as the set of
197
      features that may be included in focus instructions during training, consisting of the causal feature
198
      and the set of spurious features F = {C} ∪ S.
199
200   For a sample (x, y) ∼ pdata (x, y), we specify the focus label yfocus = yfocus (x, y, Ifocus ) ∈ Y that
      depends on the ground truth label y and focus instruction Ifocus ∈ Ifocus . Intuitively, we define focus
201
      label yfocus as yfocus = y when either no focus features are specified (i.e., using the empty focus
202   instruction), when the focus is on the underlying causal feature C, or when ignoring a spurious
203   feature S; but when either the focus is on a spurious feature or the causal feature is ignored, yfocus
204   is defined as the label spuriously correlated with a particular value of the spurious feature present
205   in input x. This changing target yfocus trains the model to learn to adjust its responses based on
      specified features. Formally, we define yfocus as:
206
207
                           
                           y
                                       if Ifocus ∈ {∅, focus(C), focus(C) ∧ ignore(S), ignore(S) | S ∈ S};
208                yfocus = ys          if Ifocus ∈ {focus(S), focus(S) ∧ ignore(Fj ) | Fj ∈ F \ {S}}, for s ∈ x;                                        (2)
209                        
                           y           if Ifocus ∈ {ignore(C)} for sampled feature S ∈ S with value s ∈ x.
                              s
210
211       1
            Examples of focus instructions specified in natural language include: “Make sentiment the central factor in
212   your decision” and “Base your prediction solely on the presence of keywords. Exclude the logical relationship
213   between the premise and hypothesis”.
          2
214         For simplicity, we focus on classification here; but FIT is also applicable to generative tasks, as we show
215   in our question-answering experiments.
          3
            Note that we use uppercase to denote features, and lowercase to denote specific values of features.


                                                                                 4
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


216
      where we again use s ∈ x to denote the presence of the feature value s in input x. See Figure 2
217   for a concrete example showing the focus label values for an example from the MNLI dataset under
218   different focus instructions.
219
220
      The objective of FIT training is to minimise the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the response yfocus
      conditioned on I, Ifocus , x. Formally, we define the FT loss objective as:
221
222                   min E(x,y)∼pdata (x,y), Ifocus ∼pIfocus (Ifocus ) [− log pθ (yfocus | I, Ifocus , x)] .   (3)
                        θ
223
224   We define pIfocus (Ifocus ) by placing a small probability mass on the empty focus instruction prompt
225
      ∅ in order to aid in learning the underlying task, and then uniformly distribute the remaining prob-
      ability mass over the remaining non-empty feature instructions. The objective in Equation (3) can
226
      be optimised through sampling using stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with popular optimisers
227
      such as AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019). Further details on FT optimisation are provided in
228   Appendix A.1.
229
230   Evaluating FIT under spurious correlations. After introducing FIT above, we now turn to settings
231
      where we can empirically train and evaluate it. A key aspect of our evaluation is the use of known
      spurious correlations, which simulate real-world scenarios where models can be misled by features
232
      that are spuriously predictive of the output label. By adjusting the co-occurrence rate between
233
      spurious features and their associated labels, we can test FIT’s ability to dynamically steer a model’s
234   responses depending on the features on which it is focusing or ignoring.
235
236   We define the co-ocurrence rate, or predictivity (Hermann et al., 2024), between spurious feature
237
      values and the label with which they are spuriously correlated by ρspurious . Specifically:
238   Definition 1. (Defining ρspurious ). Let S ∈ S ⊆ F denote a spurious feature. Suppose that a value
239   of S, say s, is spuriously correlated with label ys . Then we define ρspurious (s) as
240                                   ρspurious (s) = P(Y = ys |X, S = s, s ∈ X)                                (4)
241
      for some dataset sample (x, y) ∈ D, where S ∈ X denotes the presence of feature S in example X.
242
243   By varying ρspurious (s), we can control the predictivity of spurious features and observe the model’s
244   behaviour when focusing on or ignoring these features as well as causal features.
245
      Given a task with N classes, we require ρspurious = 1/N within the training set, ensuring that the
246
      underlying label distribution, p(y|I, Ifocus , x), is of maximum entropy when focusing on spurious
247   features. This allows the model to better distinguish between causal and spurious features, as effec-
248   tively minimising Equation (3) would require the model to make predictions without relying on the
249   underlying causal feature when its attention is specified to focus on on spurious features. A more
250   detailed exploration of this setting of ρspurious during training can be found in Appendix A.1.
251
      Next, we evaluate FIT across several test sets that capture different conditions of spurious correla-
252
      tions and distribution shifts:
253
254          • Diid : Held-out test samples with the same ρspurious as in the training set.
255          • Dhigh : Test samples with a higher ρspurious than in the training set.
256
             • Dlow : Test samples with a lower ρspurious than in the training set.
             • Dflipped : Test samples where spurious feature values are flipped to co-occur with different
257
               labels than in the training set, with the same high ρspurious as in Dhigh .
258
259   We further evaluate FIT under distribution shifts, where the specific values taken by spurious features
260   do not overlap between the training and test sets, by introducing one additional test set:
261          • Dshift (ρspurious ): Test datasets where the spurious feature values are distinct from those
262            within the training set.
263
      We evaluate over these datasets specifically on our SMNLI datset (c.f. Section 4.2).
264
265
266   4   E XPERIMENTS
267
268   In this section we empirically validate the effectiveness of FIT across a range of popular LLMs
269   of varying sizes and on different NLP datasets, including classification and multi-choice question-
      answering tasks.


                                                                5
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


270
      Before reporting the main results, we introduce the evaluation metric (focus accuracy) that we re-
271   port, baselines, models, and training settings used throughout the experiments. In Section 4.1, we
272   first verify that FIT performs well on the simpler SS dataset, a synthetic sentiment analysis dataset
273   derived from SST-5 (Socher et al., 2013b). We then demonstrate in Section 4.2 that FIT generalises
274   to more complex features and handles distribution shifts on the SMNLI dataset, a sub-sampled ver-
275   sion of the MNLI dataset (Williams et al., 2018). Finally, in Section 4.3, we show that FIT has
276   practical, real-world impact by effectively mitigating bias in the BBQ dataset (Parrish et al., 2022),
277   where we further illustrate FIT’s ability to generalise to new features seen for the first time when
278   performing inference.
279   Metrics. We define the focus accuracy for a focus instruction Ifocus ∈ Ifocus as the proportion of
280   samples where the model’s prediction aligns with the focus label, yfocus , as specified in Equation (2).
281   Specifically, for each sample (x, y) ∈ D, the model produces a prediction ŷ ∼ pθ (y | I, Ifocus , x)
282   based on a fixed focus instruction Ifocus ∈ Ifocus . The focus label, yfocus = yfocus (x, y, Ifocus ), cor-
283   responds to the target output given the focus instruction for the input x with ground truth label y.
284   Focus accuracy for focus instruction Ifocus , denoted Afocus (Ifocus ), is computed as the fraction of
285
      correct predictions with respect to the focus label:
286                                                    1 X
287
                                   Afocus (Ifocus ) =            1(ŷ = yfocus ),                             (5)
                                                      |D|
                                                             (x,y)∈D
288
289   where 1(ŷ = yfocus ) is the indicator function that equals 1 if the model’s prediction ŷ matches the
290   focus label yfocus , and 0 otherwise.
291
292
      We report focus accuracy for each model on all dataset splits, using the prompt types and focus
      instructions detailed in Appendix A.3. Generations are evaluated through simple pattern matching
293
      due to the use of constrained beam decoding. Further details are provided in Appendix A.2.
294
295   Models and training settings.            We evaluate FIT using three popular LLMs that
296   span a range of model sizes:            Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct (Dubey et al., 2024),
297   Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 (Jiang et al., 2023), and Vicuna-13B-v1.5 (Chiang et al.,
298
      2023). The models are fine-tuned using parameter-efficient SFT with LoRA (Hu et al., 2021), lever-
      aging Hugging Face’s SFTTrainer (Wolf et al., 2020) with default hyperparameters. Early stop-
299
      ping is applied based on validation loss, as defined in Equation (3). For generation, we use con-
300
      strained beam decoding (Anderson et al., 2017) and use fully verbalised (natural language) labels
301   during both training and testing, except for the multi-choice BBQ dataset. For further training de-
302   tails, refer to Appendix A.1.
303
304
      Baselines. We compare against in the main section of the paper: a few-shot baseline (Manikandan
      et al., 2023) and a SFT baseline. The SFT baseline, SFT(yfocus ), follows the same setup as the FIT
305
      method (trained on sampled inputs and focus labels), but without the inclusion of focus instructions
306
      during training. This ensures a fair comparison between FIT and the baseline, as both methods are
307   trained on the same examples and labels (i.e., focus labels yfocus ), with the only difference being
308   the inclusion of focus instructions in FIT. This setup allows us to isolate and evaluate the specific
309   impact of incorporating focus instructions in FIT. The few-shot baseline involves using 5 in-context
310   examples uniformly sampled at random from the training set for each test example, where we use
311   the same focus instruction for each in-context sample as for the test sample. In Appendix A.5, we
312   include two additional baselines: zero-shot and vanilla SFT for a more complete comparison with
313   FIT. Further details of baselines can be found in Appendix A.4.
314
315   4.1   VALIDATION OF FIT ON THE SS DATASET
316
317   Spurious Sentiment dataset (SS). We first evaluate FIT on a synthetic binary sentiment analysis
318
      dataset. Starting with SST-5 (Socher et al., 2013a), a 5-class sentiment analysis dataset, we use
      Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct (Dubey et al., 2024) to inject the spurious keywords Pineapple
319
      and Bayesian into all dataset examples in a natural way.4 In this process, we preserve the original
320
      sentiment of the dataset examples and combine categories of positive and negative labels into single
321
322      4
           We observe that the LLM makes minimal changes to each input, sometimes only inserting the keyword
323   where appropriate, and in other cases only adding a few words to create a more appropriate context (e.g.,
      prepending “According to our Bayesian analysis,” to a declarative clause). See Appendix A.7 for further details.

341
342
343   Figure 3: SS focus accuracies (↑). Focus accuracy (Afocus ) of models after SFT and FIT on the SS
344   dataset. Here, C refers to the causal feature (sentiment) and S the spurious feature (presence of one
345   of the keywords of “Bayesian” and “Pineapple”).
346
347
      classes, and exclude examples with neutral labels from our augmented dataset. The feature set
348
      is given as F = {sentiment, presence of keywords [“Bayesian”, “Pineapple”]}. We inject these
349
      features so that the presence of “Pineapple” and “Bayesian” are spuriously correlated with negative
350   and positive sentiment, respectively. The degree of co-occurrence is governed by ρspurious , which
351   varies according to the test sets described in Section 3. We ensure that ρspurious is the same for all
352   feature values within each dataset split. In particular, we set ρspurious to be 0.5, 0.5, 0.9, 0.25 and 0.9
353   on Dtrain , Diid , Dhigh , Dlow and Dflipped respectively. Further details of the SS dataset can be found in
354   Appendix A.7.
355
      Results. Figure 3 shows the focus accuracy results of the three LLMs on the SS dataset after
356   SFT and after FIT. We see that across all focus instructions and all models, FIT shows significant
357   improvement over the baselines, achieving very high focus accuracy.
358
359         Key takeaways. High focus accuracy on SS indicates that FIT training successfully allows
360         the model to alter its response by considering the feature on which it is instructed to focus
361         or not focus. This shows that the model’s behaviour can be effectively steered using FIT.
362
363
      4.2     FIT PERFORMS WELL WITH MORE COMPLEX FEATURES ON THE SMNLI DATASET AND
364
              GENERALISES UNDER DISTRIBUTION SHIFT
365
366   Spurious MNLI dataset (SMNLI). Next, we evaluate our method on a more complex dataset
367   with subtler features. Specifically, we construct an NLI dataset by sub-sampling from MNLI
368   (Williams et al., 2018), where we induce a spurious correlation between text genres and labels by
369   sub-sampling accordingly. We refer to this dataset as SMNLI, where the feature set is defined as
370   F = {NLI relationship, genre}. The co-occurrence rate of genres and their spuriously associated
371   label is governed by ρspurious , which varies across the test sets discussed in Section 3. We ensure that
372   ρspurious is the same for all feature values within each dataset split. In particular, we set ρspurious to be
373   1/3, 1/3, 0.9, 0.1 and 0.9 on Dtrain , Diid , Dhigh , Dlow and Dflipped respectively.
374   Moreover, for SMNLI, we hold out specific genres at test time to evaluate our model’s ability to
375   generalise under distribution shift when feature values change. We do this by sub-sampling a held-
376   out portion of the MNLI dataset. During training, we use three selected genres (government, fiction,
377   and travel) to evaluate our models. At test time, we add an additional three held-out genres (faceto-
      face, nineeleven, and verbatim). We again ensure that ρspurious is constant within each dataset split

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             iid


396
397   Figure 4: SMNLI focus accuracies (↑). Focus accuracy (Afocus ) of models after SFT and FIT on
398   the SMNLI dataset. Here, C refers to the causal feature (logical relationship between premise and
399   hypothesis) and S the spurious feature (genre of the underlying text)
400
401
      for all feature values, and use the same set of corresponding ρspurious as within the SMNLI test sets
402
      described above. Further details of the SMNLI dataset can be found in Appendix A.9. Results.
403   Figure 4 depicts the focus accuracy results of the three models on the SMNLI test splits. We observe
404   that even for the more complex feature of genre, FIT achieves very high focus accuracy, signif-
405   icantly improving over the baselines. This demonstrates that FIT effectively trains the model to
406   handle more complex features, allowing it to dynamically focus on or disregard these features when
407   making predictions.
408
      Figure 5 shows the focus accuracy of models on the distribution-shifted test sets across different
409   values of ρspurious . When focusing on the causal feature or ignoring the spurious feature, the model
410   maintains strong performance in terms of focus accuracy, even on unseen genre values (over 80%
411   focus accuracy for FIT models on the second row of Figure 5). Note that, while we observe low focus
412   accuracy when focusing on spurious features, this is expected, as the spurious labels associated with
413   these new genres were not encountered during training. Thus when focusing on these features the
414   model does not know what label to predict. This result highlights that the focus-tuned models have
415   indeed learned spurious associations during training and correctly reproduces them when instructed
416   to focus on these spurious features, even for new spurious feature values. When instructed to focus
417
      on the causal feature (or even just to ignore the spurious feature), the model still shows strong
      generalisation in the presence of distribution shift.
418
419
            Key takeaways. FIT achieves high focus accuracy on more complex features and maintains
420         strong performance under distribution shift in terms of feature values. This demonstrates
421         FIT’s ability to generalise to new contexts and reliably handle changing feature values, which
422         is crucial for ensuring consistent and robust model performance in dynamic settings.
423
424
      4.3       FIT STEERS BEHAVIOUR IN THE PRESENCE OF SOCIAL BIAS DATA AND GENERALISES TO
425
                UNSEEN FEATURES
426
427   Bias Benchmark for QA (BBQ) dataset. Finally, we experiment with BBQ Parrish et al. (2022), a
428   widely-utilised multiple-choice question-answering benchmark annotated with nine forms of so-
429   cial bias that are relevant to any given answer, such as stereotypes that would imply a given
430   answer to an otherwise ambiguous question (see Figure 1). The feature set contains F =
431   {question context, gender identity, race/ethnicity, ..., disability status}, which contains one causal
      feature (question context) and 9 bias features. Of the n = 9 bias features, we focus-tune mod-

      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

                                                                          shift



450
451   Figure 5: SMNLI focus accuracies (↑) under distribution shift . Focus accuracy (Afocus ) of models
452   after SFT and FIT on SMNLI, evaluated on distribution-shifted test sets with different feature values
453   (genres) from training. shift(ρspurious ) refers to test sets where feature values (genres) differ from
454
      training with a co-occurrence rate of ρspurious . Here, “flipped” indicates a change in spurious label
      associations between training and testing, with high ρspurious in the test set.
455
456
457
      els with respect to 6, and test on these 6 features plus the remaining 3 bias features in order to
458   test how well FIT generalises to features that are not seen during focus tuning. Here, we consider
459   the spurious features to be the presence of a particular social group (e.g., men or women) in the
460   question context, and spurious answers to be those that would be indicated by relying on social
461   stereotypes rather than the specific question context (e.g., see Figure 1). The stereotyped response
462   used to determine spurious answers for these bias features are provided as part of the BBQ dataset.
463
      Results. Figure 6 shows the focus accuracy results of the three models on the BBQ dataset, visualis-
464   ing performance on features seen during training and unseen, held-out features. The models demon-
465   strate high and comparable focus accuracy across both seen and unseen bias features, indicating that
466   FIT generalises well to unseen features, including nuanced reasoning about group stereotypes. This
467   highlights the usefulness of FIT in mitigating social biases in LLM responses. Specifically, FIT can
468   effectively learn, reason about, and rule out biases when formulating responses, making it a practical
469   tool for bias mitigation.
470
471       Key takeaways. FIT can effectively teach models to adjust their responses based on knowl-
472       edge of social biases. This ability generalises to biases not seen during FIT training, indicat-
473
          ing FIT’s utility for bias mitigation.
474
475
      5   A BLATION
476
477
      Generalisation to different test-time prompt formats. As observed in the IT literature,
478
      instruction-tuned models sometimes memorise instruction formats and struggle to follow para-
479
      phrased instructions at test time (Ghosh et al., 2024). In Appendix A.6 (Figure 8), we compare
480   the performance of models on the SMNLI dataset when using the same focus instructions at training
481   and test time versus using paraphrased instructions at test time. We generate 10 different test-time
482   focus instructions of each instruction type defined in Equation (1) by paraphrasing the existing focus
483   instruction using ChatGPT (OpenAI, 2022). The results show minimal variation in focus accuracy
484   across different dataset splits and focus features, even when testing on paraphrased prompts, indi-
485   cating that FIT indeed teaches models a general capacity to focus on or ignore features regardless of
      the specific way that focus instructions are phrased.

508
509
510   6   C ONCLUSIONS
511
512   In this work, we introduce Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT), a method designed to steer the behaviour
513   of LLMs by focusing on or ignoring specific features when formulating responses. Across a range
514   of tasks and settings, we demonstrate that FIT can be used to steer LLM behaviours at inference
515   time, even in the context of distribution shifts over feature values or when generalizing to unseen
516   features at inference time. Additionally, we show that our method can mitigate biases by identifying
517   and factoring out known stereotypes that might otherwise influence responses. Thus, FIT represents
518   a step toward enabling more robust, fair, and controllable LLMs.
519   We recommend that future work explore the effectiveness of FIT across a broader variety of tasks,
520   including open-ended, free-form natural language generation tasks such as summarization or trans-
521   lation. Another promising direction is investigating whether FIT can generalise not only across
522   features but also across different categories of tasks (cf. FLAN; Longpre et al. 2023).
523
524
525   E THICAL C ONSIDERATIONS AND L IMITATIONS
526
527
      The ability to dynamically steer model behaviour by focusing on or ignoring features, as enabled by
528   FIT, holds significant potential for reducing algorithmic discrimination and mitigating harms. Prac-
529   titioners can leverage FIT to identify and correct biases by measuring discrepancies in behaviour
530   when a model focuses on or ignores specific features. Additionally, FIT enhances explainability by
531   attributing model predictions to input features, enabling more transparent and productive human-AI
532   collaboration. This supports ethical and responsible decision-making by assessing whether predic-
533   tions are justified. FIT also enhances robustness by prioritising stable causal features expected to
534   generalise across domains while ignoring spurious, domain-specific biases, making it a valuable tool
535   for fairness, explainability, and robustness.
536   However, risks include potential misuse by bad actors to bias models, though this is not unique to FIT
537   and could already be achieved through biased fine-tuning. Additionally, as noted in Appendix A.10,
538   FIT may face challenges when addressing features that overlap heavily or lack distinctiveness. While
539   these constraints may arise in specific contexts, they do not diminish FIT’s broader applicability
      across natural-language tasks.

804   A     A PPENDIX

806   A.1   FT T RAINING AND O PTIMISATION S ETTINGS
807
808   FT Optimisation. Algorithm 1 gives precise details on how we implement FIT in practice when per-
809   forming SFT of a model on a given training set. In particular, it shows how we approach optimising
      the FIT training objective given in Equation (3).


                                                      15
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


810
      Algorithm 1 Algorithm for Focus Instruction Tuning (FIT) Training Procedure to Optimise Equa-
811   tion (3).
812
        1: Input: Dataset D = {(xi , yi )}N     i=1 , The feature set contains F, instruction I, model pa-
813
           rameters θ, batch size B, number of epochs E, step size η, and mapping function yfocus =
814        yfocus (x, y, Ifocus ).
815     2: Initialise: Model parameters θ, optimiser
816     3: for epoch = 1 to E do
817     4:    for mini-batch {(xb , y b )}B
                                          b=1 from D do
                                b b
818     5:       for each (x , y ) in the mini-batch do
                                                b
819     6:          Sample focus instruction Ifocus  ∼ pIfocus (Ifocus )
                                   b           b b b
820     7:          Compute yfocus = yfocus (x , y , Ifocus )
821     8:       end for
822     9:       Compute average loss given through empirical estimator of the loss defined in Equation (3)
823              over the batch:
                                                        B
824                                                 1 X                  b        b
                                           L(θ) =           − log pθ (yfocus |I, Ifocus , xb )
825                                                B
                                                    b=1
826

831   12: end for
832   13: Output: Optimised model parameters θ
833
834
835
836
837
838
839
840
841
842
843
844
      FT training settings. We use the SFTTrainer class from HuggingFace (Wolf et al., 2020) and use
845   all of the default training settings for performing SFT of LLMs. Furthermore, we define p(Ifocus )
846   by placing a small probability (in our experiments, 0.05) on the empty focus instruction ∅. We then
847   uniformly distribute the remaining probability mass over the non-empty focus instructions.
848
      We implement early stopping on a held-out validation set based on the cross-entropy loss over focus
849
      labels yfocus corresponding to randomly sampled focus instructions - this matches the context in
850
      which the models will be evaluated. We obtain this set by splitting our training set in an 80/20% for
851   training and validation. We use a patience of 4 validation evaluation steps, which occur after a fixed
852   number of steps.
853
854
      We use LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning. We target the query and value
      projection matrices within each LLM and use LoRA r = 16 and α = 32 across models.
855
856   Choice of ρspurious during training. Consider a classification problem with N classes so that |Y| =
857   6. During FIT training we want the model to learn to change it’s behaviour depending on which
858   features are specified to be focused on or ignored.
859   To achieve this, consider when the focus instruction requires that the model focus on a spurious
860   feature during training, which means that Ifocus ∈ {focus(S), focus(S) ∧ ignore(Fj ) | Fj ∈ F \
861   {S}}, we choose ρspurious = 1/N. The definition of ρspurious given in Equation (4) implies that in this
862   setting ρspurious = P(Y = ys |X, I, Ifocus , s ∈ X) for a feature value s of spurious feature S in input
863   X. Therefore, setting ρspurious = 1/N for the training dataset induces a uniform distribution over the
      set the set of class labels conditioned on an input X and focus instruction Ifocus during training.


                                                          16
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


864
      The entropy of this discrete distribution is then given by:
865
866
867
868
869    H(P(Y = ys |X, I,Ifocus , s ∈ X)) =                                                               (6)
870                      = Ey∼P(Y =ys |X,I,Ifocus ,s∈X) [− log P(Y = ys |X, I, Ifocus , s ∈ X)]          (7)
871                             X
872                      =−          P(Y = ys |X, I, Ifocus , s ∈ X) log P(Y = ys |X, I, Ifocus , s ∈ X) (8)
873                               y∈Y

874
                                  X      1         1
                            =−       log                                                                    (9)
875                                      N         N
                                y∈Y
876
877
                                       1
                            = − log                                                                        (10)
878                                    N
879                         = log N.                                                                       (11)
880
881
882
883
884
      It is well known that discrete uniform distributions have maximum entropy (Bishop & Nasrabadi,
      2006).
885
886   Practically, this means that the causal feature is not predictive of the focus label, which is what we are
887   training the model to predict. A lower-entropy distribution (compared to the uniform distribution)
888   during training would result in a higher co-occurrence of the spurious labels with the underlying
889   causal labels. This could lead the model to rely on the underlying task-causal feature to solve the
890
      task, rat her than learning to adapt its behaviour when focusing on a non-causal feature. Therefore,
      using the maximum-entropy uniform distribution during training better enables the model to learn
891
      to adjust its behaviour based on the specified features. This ensures that the model does not fall back
892
      on causal features when spurious ones are the focus, thus improving the steerability of the model.
893
894
895
896
897
898   A.2    E VALUATION M ETRICS
899
900
901   Generation settings. We generate responses from our FT model using constrained beam-decoding
902   (Anderson et al., 2017) with 8 beams. This ensures that the answer labels for each classification task
903   that we investigate appear in the model’s output. We limit the maximum number of newly generated
904   tokens to be 5 to stop any unnecessary text given after the model’s initial classification prediction.
905   Computing the focus accuracy metric. We report the accuracy of generations when evaluating
906   FT models. As we are guaranteed to include the task labels within the model’s response through
907   constrained decoding, we simply check to see if the focus label, yfocus , is within the model’s response
908   or not in order to determine if the model’s response is correct.
909
910
911
912
913
914   A.3    FIT F OCUS I NSTRUCTIONS AND P ROMPT T EMPLATES
915
916
917   Focus instructions. We consider the following focus instruction formats for the different focus
      instructions introduced in Equation (1) which are used for FIT training and evaluation:


                                                         17
      Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


918
         Focus instructions Ifocus
919
920      For features Fi , Fj ∈ F :
921
         Focus instructions focus(Fi ):
922
                  • Direct your attention solely to Fi .
923
                  • Concentrate all your reasoning on Fi .
924
                  • Make Fi the central factor in your decision.
925
                  • Base your judgment exclusively on Fi .
926
                  • Pay attention only to Fi when making your prediction.
927
                  • Use Fi as the key input for your evaluation.
928
                  • Focus entirely on Fi and ignore other aspects.
929
                  • Rely exclusively on Fi to reach your conclusion.
930
                  • Consider only Fi and disregard all else.
931
                  • Let Fi be the primary basis for your decision.
932
933      Ignore instructions ignore(Fi ):
934               • Completely rule out Fi from your reasoning.
935               • Disregard any influence of Fi in your prediction.
936               • Treat Fi as irrelevant to your decision-making process.
937               • Exclude Fi entirely from your evaluation.
938               • Do not let Fi play any role in your assessment.
939               • Intervene to prevent Fi from affecting your prediction.
940               • Ensure that Fi has no bearing on your final decision.
941               • Block Fi from contributing to your reasoning.
942               • Negate the impact of Fi in your prediction.
943               • Ruling out Fi is crucial—do not let it affect your decision.
944
945      Focus and Ignore instructions focus(Fi ) ∧ ignore(Fj )

946               • Focus specifically on Fi . Disregard Fj in your decision-making process.

947               • Base your prediction solely on Fi . Exclude Fj .

948               • Direct all your attention to Fi . Block out Fj from your prediction.

949               • Consider only Fi in your reasoning. Rule out Fj in your decision-making.

950               • Prioritize Fi . Completely ignore Fj in your prediction.

951               • Do not consider Fj in your decision-making process. Focus exclusively on Fi .

952               • Ignore any influence of Fj . Concentrate on Fi in your prediction.

953               • Disregard Fj entirely. Base your analysis solely on Fi .

954               • Rule out Fj in your prediction. Shift your focus to Fi .

955               • Do not pay attention to Fj in your decision-making process. Rely only on Fi .

956
957   Prompt template for SS. We consider the following prompt templates for the SS dataset:
958
959      SS Focus instruction prompt templates Ifocus
960
         <INSTRUCTION>
961      You are a language model performing sentiment analysis on a binary dataset, making predictions from the labels [negative,
962      positive]. Make your prediction based on the relevant features described below, focusing on the specified features and ignoring
         those deemed irrelevant. For the input below, output either negative or positive ONLY for your prediction of the input’s label.
963      <END OF INSTRUCTION>
964
         <FEATURE CONSIDERATIONS>
965      {focus instruction - Ifocus }
966      <END FEATURE CONSIDERATIONS>
967      ### Input:       {input}
968
         ### Output:
969
970
971   where for the instruction with no focus instruction, ∅, we simply drop the feature considerations
      section and focus instruction from the prompt.


                                                                        18
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


972
       Prompt template for SMNLI. We consider the following prompt templates for the SMNLI dataset.
973
974        NLI focus instruction prompt template Ifocus
975
           <INSTRUCTION>
976        You are a language model performing natural language inference (NLI) on a dataset with three labels [entailment, neutral,
977        contradiction]. For the inputs consisting of premise and hypothesis below, make your prediction based on the relevant features
           described, focusing on the specified features and ignoring those deemed irrelevant. Output either entailment, neutral, or
978        contradiction ONLY for your prediction of the input’s label.
979        <END OF INSTRUCTION>
980        <FEATURE CONSIDERATIONS>
981        {focus instruction - Ifocus }
           <END FEATURE CONSIDERATIONS>
982
983        ### Premise: {premise}
           ### Hypothesis: {hypothesis}
984
985        ### Output:
986
987    where we again drop the feature considerations section and focus instruction from the prompt to
988    form the instruction prompt ∅ with no focus instruction.
989    BBQ focus instruction prompt template Ifocus We consider the following prompt templates for the
990    BBQ dataset.
991
992        BBQ focus instruction prompt template Ifocus
993
           <INSTRUCTION>
994        You are a language model answering a QUESTION based on a provided CONTEXT, choosing to ANSWER with one of three
995        options: 1, 2, or 3. Each option corresponds to an answer from the provided ANSWER OPTIONS. You must output EXACTLY
           one of these numbers (1, 2, or 3) representing the answer you select.
996        <END OF INSTRUCTION>
997
           <FEATURE CONSIDERATIONS>
998        {focus instruction - Ifocus }
999        <END FEATURE CONSIDERATIONS>

1000       ### CONTEXT: {context}
1001
           ### QUESTION: {question}
1002
1003       ### ANSWER OPTIONS:
           (1) {ans0}
1004       (2) {ans1}
1005       (3) {ans2}

1006       ### ANSWER:
1007
1008   where we again drop the feature considerations to get the template for the focus instruction ∅.
1009
1010   A.4    BASELINES
1011
1012   We include results for the following two baselines to fruther supplement the results presented in the
1013   main section of the paper.
1014   SFT(yfocus ) baseline. We implement an SFT baseline that follows the same training procedure as
1015   FIT, except during training, we exclude any focus instructions from the input prompts while still
1016   training on the focus labels. This provides a fair comparison with FIT, as the models are trained on
1017   the same input text and label pairs. The rest of the training setup, including hyperparameters and
1018   early stopping, remains identical to the FIT training setup.The model is tested on the full set of focus
1019   instructions prompts detailed in Equation (1).
1020   Few-shot baseline. This second baseline compares FIT training to few-shot inference using the
1021   original pre-trained models without additional fine-tuning on our spurious datasets. Specifically, we
1022   use 5 in-context examples across all datasets. For the in-context examples, we concatenate multiple
1023   examples one after the other, including the instructional prompt only for the first in-context example
1024   and the final test example. Each in-context example contains the same focus instruction as the test
1025   example for which they serve as context. The model is tested on the full set of focus instructions
       prompts detailed in Equation (1).


                                                                        19
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1026
       For completeness, we report two additional baselines: vanilla SFT and zero-shot baselines.
1027
1028   SFT(y) baseline. We implement a vanilla SFT baseline that simply trains a model using SFT on
1029   inputs and their ground truth labels (as opposed to focus labels in the SFT(yfocus ) baseline). During
1030
       training, only standard IT prompts are used, with no additional focus instructions included. The
       rest of the training setup, including hyperparameters and early stopping, remains identical to the
1031
       FIT training setup. The model is tested on the full set of focus instructions prompts detailed in
1032
       Equation (1).
1033
1034   Zero-shot baseline. Finally, we include a zero-shot inference baseline using the original pre-trained
1035   models without additional fine-tuning on our spurious datasets. No in-context examples are used at
1036
       inference time, and the model is not trained at all beyond it’s pre-training. The model is tested on
       the full set of focus instructions prompts detailed in Equation (1).
1037
1038

1041   dataset to further supplement the results in Section 4.2.

1060   Figure 7: Focus accuracy (↑) for zero-shot and SFT(y) on SMNLI. Figure giving focus accuracies
1061   (Afocus ) of the additional zero-shot and SFT(y) baselines on the SMNLI dataset.

1064   A.6                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         SMNLI A BLATION OF T RAINING AND T EST TIME F OCUS I NSTRUCTION R EPHRASING
1065                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               D IFFERENCES
1066
1067   We analyse the impact of using the same versus different sets of focus instructions at training and
1068
       test time when applying FIT models. Specifically, we generate alternative test set focus instructions
       by paraphrasing the training focus instructions, as shown in Appendix A.3, using ChatGPT.
1069
1070   As depicted in Figure 8, the results of this ablation reveal negligible differences between using the
1071   same or different focus instruction phrasings during training and testing. This indicates that FIT
1072   effectively trains the model to focus on or ignore features, regardless of how the instructions are
1073   phrased.
1074
1075   A.7                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         S PURIOUS S ENTIMENT (SS) DATASET
1076
       We take a pre-existing dataset, in this case SST-5 (Socher et al., 2013a), and modify it in order to
1077
       induce a known spurious feature and create a spurious binary sentiment analysis dataset.
1078
1079   Data-generating process (DGP). We frame our DGP using a graphical model to describe the syn-
       thetic dataset that we create. We follow a similar model to that described in (Arjovsky et al., 2019),

       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025

1107
1108
       Figure 9: SS DGP. Graphical model showing the data generating process for modifying examples
1109
       from the SST-5 dataset to introduce a new spurious keyword feature S.
1110
1111
1112   specifically the model used for generating their coloured MNIST dataset. We use the following
1113   variables within our graphical model:
1114
1115                 • C - true underlying sentiment, the causal feature within this task, sampled from the original
1116                   dataset.
1117                 • S - spurious feature, here this is the presence of the keywords Bayesian or Pineapple. We
1118                   represent this as a binary vector S ∈ {0, 1}2 , where the first and second components of this
1119                   vector denote the presence of either the keyword Pineapple or Bayesian respectively.
1120
                     • X̃ - is a sampled example from the original dataset that we are modifying to inject known
1121
                       spurious correlations.
1122
1123                 • X - original example X̃ but augmented to include the spurious feature.
1124                 • Y - final label for element X.
1125
1126   The graphical model describing the DGP of the SS dataset is given in Figure 9. This admits a
1127   functional representation in the form:
1128

                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         (15)
1133
                                                                                  Y = fY (C, UY ).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    21
       Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2025


1134
       where U(·) are variables introducing sources of randomness into the generating process. More ex-
1135   plicitly, we consider the following set of equations, where D denotes the underlying dataset that we
1136   are manipulating:
1137
                       C ∼ Ber(ρC ), where ρC = ρC (D);                                                (17)
1138
1139                   X̃ ∼ pD (·|C) ,                                                                    (18)
1140                   S = (1C=0 , 1C=1 ) ;                                                               (19)
1141                   Uincld. ∼ Ber(ρincld. );                                                           (20)
1142
                       UX ∼ Ber(ρspurious );                                                              (21)
1143
1144                            UX LLM(X̃, S) + (1 − UX )LLM(X̃, 1 − S) if Uincd. = 1
                       X=                                                              ;                  (22)
1145                            X̃                                       if Uincd. = 0
1146                    Y = C,                                                                           (23)
1147
       The variable ρC gives the distribution of sentiment labels in the original binarised SST-5 dataset.
1148   Moreover, pD (·|C) denotes the conditional dataset distribution of the different input texts give C
1149   (here we assume that we are just uniformly sampling text with the given sentiment C) and 1(·)
1150   denotes the indicator function. In addition, ρincl. gives the proportion of spurious features that are
1151   included within original dataset examples. This corresponds to proportion of examples within the
1152   original dataset that are modified by the above process and therefore contain spurious feature values.
1153
       Finally, we prove that ρspurious gives the concurrence rate between the label Y and the spurious feature
1154   values of S. The proof rests on the fact that UX , which gives whether a spurious feature value s is
1155   injected into X̃ or whether the other value 1−s is injected instead, controls the cooccurrence between
1156
       Y and the spurious feature value s. In particular, we note that P(Y = y, UX = 1, X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = s)
1157   gives the co-occurrence rate between the label y and spurious feature s in the dataset (assuming that
1158   if the feature is only present within the dataset through the inclusion of the feature and not within
1159   the original dataset examples).
1160   Proposition 1. From the DGP described in Figure 9, we have that
1161
1162
                          P(Y = 1, UX = 1, X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = (0, 1)) = ρincdl. · ρspurious ;                (24)
1163                      P(Y = 0, UX = 1, X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = (1, 0)) = ρincdl. · ρspurious .                (25)
1164
1165   Proof. Using the chain rule of probability, we see that
1166      P(Y = 1, UX = 1, X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = (0, 1)) =                                                      (26)
1167
1168         = P(Y = 1|UX = 1, X̃, X ̸= X̃, S = (0, 1))P(UX = 1, X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = (0, 1))                  (27)
1169         = P(Y = 1|S = (0, 1))P(UX = 1, X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = (0, 1))                                       (28)
1170
             = P(Y = 1|S = (0, 1))P(UX = 1|X̃, X ̸= X̃, S = (0, 1))P(X ̸= X̃|X̃, S = (0, 1))              (29)
1171
1172         = P(Y = 1|S = (0, 1))P(UX = 1)P(X ̸= X̃|X̃)                                           (30)
1173         = P(Y = 1|C = 1)P(UX = 1)P(Uincld. = 1)                                               (31)
1174         = 1 · ρincdl. · ρspurious                                                             (32)
1175         = ρincdl. · ρspurious ,                                                               (33)
1176
       where we have used that S and C share a deterministic relationship and have used the conditional
1177
       independencies specified within the graphical model depicted in Figure 9, and through the noise
1178
       terms U(·) in the SCEs introduced above.
1179
1180
       With ρincld. = 1, we have that, indeed, the co-occurrence rate between the presence of spurious
1181   feature values and the labels Y is given by ρspurious .
1182
1183   We now connect this to predicitvity which is defined Equation (4) in order to justify the notation that
1184
       we are using within the SCEs above.
1185   Corollary 1. From the DGP described in Figure 11, we have that
1186                          P(Y = 1|X̃, S = (0, 1), UX = 1, X ̸= X̃) = ρspurious ;                      (34)
1187
                              P(Y = 0|X̃, S = (1, 0), UX = 1, X ̸= X̃) = ρspurious .                      (35)

1222
1223   SCM from this DGP. Through the above data generation process, we introduce a new spurious
1224   feature within the dataset S, the presence of the keywords Bayesian and Pineapple. Recalling that
1225
       S = (1, 0) and S = (0, 1) correspond to insertion of the keywords Pineapple and Bayesian re-
       spectively, we introduce the following spurious correlations between feature values of S and label
1226
       Y:
1227
1228                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       1. The presence of the word Pineapple in the text X is spuriously correlated the label 0 (neg-
1229                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          ative sentiment).
1230
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           2. The presence of the word Bayesian in the text X is spuriously correlated with the label 1
1231
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              (positive sentiment).
1232
1233   The sentiment feature still remains causal within the augmented SS dataset, fully predicting the label
1234   Y for each dataset example.
1235
1236
       The above DGP, through the introduction of spurious feature S, induces a SCM that describes the
       spurious correlation between spurious feature S and the label Y . The SCM, shown in Figure 10,
1237
       follows the style-content decomposition described in (Kaddour et al., 2022), where U is some hidden
1238
       confounding variable.
1239
1240   Data generation methodology. We use Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct to generate modifications
1241   X of original dataset examples X̃ to create new text which include the new keywords feature. The
       prompt we use for generation when modifying examples to include spurious features is give as:
